maxwell.headline: Ghislaine Maxwell invoked Fifth Amendment in House Oversight deposition while her attorney publicly appealed for Trump clemency in exchange for testimony.
maxwell.summary: On February 10, 2026, Maxwell asserted her Fifth Amendment right during a closed-door video deposition with the House Oversight Committee, refusing to answer questions on Epstein-related matters[2][3]. Her attorney stated she is prepared to testify fully if granted clemency by President Trump, emphasizing she alone can provide the complete account and that both Trump and Clinton are innocent[2][3]. The White House press secretary confirmed on the same day that a pardon is not a priority and has not been discussed recently with the president[2]. Democrats, including Rep. Jamie Raskin and Sen. Jacky Rosen, criticized the potential clemency and special treatment allegations at her Texas prison, with Rosen introducing a Senate resolution opposing any pardon[1][4]. Maxwell also has a pending habeas petition challenging her conviction[2].

iran.headline: US and Israel conducted extensive airstrikes on Iranian nuclear, missile, and leadership targets through March 3, 2026, under joint campaign including Operation Epic Fury.
iran.summary: On March 2-3, US and Israeli forces struck Iranian-backed militias in Iraq, nuclear facilities like Natanz, missile production sites, air defenses, and key Tehran leadership complexes including the Supreme National Security Council and Presidential Office, achieving air dominance over Tehran.[1] Iran retaliated with drone and missile attacks on US embassies in Gulf states (Riyadh, Dubai, Kuwait) causing closures and a fire, plus limited missile barrages on Israel injuring 12 near Tel Aviv.[1] Iranian regime delegated powers to provincial governors amid disruptions and intensified internal security with checkpoints and internet blackouts; no new developments reported after March 3 or on CRINK coordination, Strait of Hormuz, succession, or tracked entities like Mojtaba Khamenei or Ghalibaf.[1]

gulf_swf.headline: Saudi PIF soft-launches 2026-2030 strategy at Riyadh forum, shifting focus to AI, industry, minerals, and tourism while scaling back mega-projects.
gulf_swf.summary: Saudi Arabia's Public Investment Fund (PIF) soft-launched its new five-year strategy for 2026-2030 with key investors and partners on the sidelines of a Riyadh conference on March 9, 2026, marking a pivot from expensive mega-projects like The Line to sectors including industry, minerals, AI, and tourism.[1][2][4] PIF Governor Yasir Al-Rumayyan announced a shift from launching opportunities to accelerating growth through private sector partnerships, offering over 190 investment opportunities worth SAR 40 billion and signing prior agreements exceeding SAR 15 billion.[2] The strategy aligns with Vision 2030's third phase, emphasizing ecosystem integration, local content spending of SAR 591 billion from 2020-2024, and attracting global capital amid fiscal pressures from low oil prices.[1][2] No new developments reported on Mubadala, MGX, ADIA, QIA, GlobalFoundries, or Affinity Partners.

oracle_ellison.headline: Oracle announced $45-50B equity and debt financing plan for 2026 to fund cloud infrastructure expansion amid high debt scrutiny.
oracle_ellison.summary: Oracle revealed plans on February 1, 2026, to raise $45-50 billion in gross cash proceeds during 2026 through a balanced mix of debt and equity, including a $20B at-the-market equity program and a one-time senior unsecured bond issuance early in the year, to support Oracle Cloud Infrastructure growth for customers like OpenAI.[1][3][4] The announcement addressed investor concerns over Oracle's $100B debt load and ties to OpenAI's data center demands, with Bank of America noting it defused a key 2026 risk by providing financing transparency.[2] No new developments on Stargate contraction, Ellison WBD acquisition, or Paramount-Skydance mergers reported as of March 9, 2026.

epstein_files.headline: DOJ released over 1,000 additional Epstein files on March 5, 2026, including more than a dozen related to Trump allegations, with the Epstein Library last updated March 7, 2026[3][4].
epstein_files.summary: The DOJ published more than 1,000 new pages to the public Epstein database on Thursday, March 5, 2026, featuring over a dozen files tied to allegations of President Trump sexually abusing a minor, following an NPR investigation identifying missing documents[3]. The Epstein Library site was last updated on March 7, 2026, hosting materials under the Epstein Files Transparency Act with ongoing redactions for victim privacy[4]. Earlier in February 2026, DOJ released over 3 million additional pages totaling nearly 3.5 million, including videos and images from multiple investigations, after initial removals of 47,000 files containing survivor information[1][2]. No new mentions of Pam Bondi subpoenas, House Oversight actions, FBI 302 withholdings, or tracked entities like Thomas Massie or Ro Khanna in recent releases.

convergence_note: 6 nodes active simultaneously — within the 7-day convergence window defined by the thermostat model.
